title: Deep Learning Book
text:
0.1 Why this book
This online book is structured for progressive learning with concise chapters, cross references, and searchable HTML pages.1

title: Deep Learning Book
text: 1 Footnote demo: hover the superscript marker to see a preview tooltip, then click for the full note.

title: Deep Learning Book
text:
0.2 How to use

Start with Part I and read chapters in order.
Use the left sidebar to jump across sections.
Use search to find concepts quickly.

title: Deep Learning Book
text:
0.3 Learning path
See Part I: Foundations.

title: 2  Foundations
text:
2.1 Core question
What are the minimum mathematical and computational concepts needed to understand modern deep learning systems?1

title: 2  Foundations
text: 1 In practice, this baseline includes linear algebra, derivatives, and optimization intuition.

title: 2  Foundations
text:
2.2 Key ideas

Vectors and matrices represent data and parameters.
Differentiable objectives define learning targets.
Computation graphs organize forward and backward passes.

title: 2  Foundations
text:
2.3 Example
We use a simple linear function:
\[
y = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b
\]
This appears repeatedly in deeper models as a local building block.

title: 2  Foundations
text:
2.4 Training geometry
As shown in Figure 2.1, optimizing parameters is equivalent to moving on a loss surface.






Figure 2.1: Illustration of a toy loss surface and gradient direction.

title: 2  Foundations
text:
2.5 Summary
Foundations create a shared language for all later chapters.

title: 3  Linear Models
text:
3.1 Problem setup
Given feature vectors \(\mathbf{x}\) and targets \(y\), fit parameters \((\mathbf{w}, b)\) to minimize prediction error.

title: 3  Linear Models
text:
3.2 Objective
For regression, the mean squared error objective is:
\[
L(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2
\]
with \(\hat{y}_i = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b\).

title: 3  Linear Models
text:
3.3 Why it matters
Linear models are simple enough to reason about and form the conceptual bridge to neural networks. For optimization intuition, revisit Figure 2.1 from the foundations chapter.

title: 4  Optimization
text:
4.1 Goal
Optimization updates parameters to reduce loss over training data.

title: 4  Optimization
text:
4.2 Gradient descent
A standard update rule is:
\[
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)
\]
where \(\eta\) is the learning rate.

title: 4  Optimization
text:
4.3 Practical notes

Too large a learning rate can diverge.
Too small a learning rate can stall progress.
Batch-based updates improve computational efficiency.

title: 4  Optimization
text:
4.4 Summary
Optimization operationalizes learning by turning objectives into parameter updates.
